segments.title: Article 8's exempt-vessel rules
segments.summary: Article 8 exempted small naval vessels, but used displacement, gun-calibre, torpedo, speed, armour, mine, and aircraft rules to stop evasions.
segments.body: This section unpacks the treaty's attempt to distinguish minor combatants and fleet auxiliaries from limited warships. Vessels at or below 600 tons were exempt, while those up to 2,000 tons had to avoid features that made them too capable. The discussion shows how the treaty treated design intent as well as fitted equipment, especially for torpedoes, speed, armour, mines, and aircraft facilities. Those detailed exclusions created the legal space in which sloops and motor torpedo boats could exist without simply becoming uncounted cruisers.

evidence.note: The treaty text sets the 600- and 2,000-ton bands and lists the prohibited characteristics.
The speakers connect Article 8 to sloops, motor torpedo boats, and other exempt types.

segments.title: Why carriers were limited to six-inch guns
segments.summary: The six-inch limit on carrier guns closed off a loophole in which a nominal flight deck could conceal an eight-inch-gun cruiser.
segments.body: The speakers explain the carrier gun limit as an anti-evasion measure, not merely an aviation rule. Without it, a navy could claim that a cruiser-sized warship was an aircraft carrier by providing only a token flight deck. The hypothetical result would have been a heavily armed fast ship that consumed carrier rather than cruiser allowance. This makes the treaty's categories easier to understand: weapon limits protected the purpose of the whole allocation system.

evidence.note: The discussion describes the risk of building a cruiser with a token flight deck and eight-inch guns.
A deliberately exaggerated carrier-cruiser hybrid illustrates the loophole being prevented.

segments.title: The six-inch threshold and small-cruiser pressure
segments.summary: Six-inch guns marked the point at which a ship became a dangerous cruiser, pressuring smaller navies not to turn destroyers into miniature cruisers.
segments.body: Here the video explains why the treaty repeatedly cares about 6.1-inch calibre. In Royal Navy thinking, that was the point at which a vessel entered cruiser territory, whereas normal destroyer and sloop armaments were well below it. The restriction therefore bore hardest on powers seeking maximum gun power in destroyer-sized hulls. The later interest in 5.9-inch weapons shows that designers could still search for room immediately below a treaty threshold.

evidence.note: The speakers identify 6.1 inches as the cruiser boundary and contrast it with Royal Navy destroyer and sloop guns.
The transcript notes interest in 5.9-inch guns as a way of pressing against the limit.

segments.title: Treaty pressure and the super-destroyer race
segments.summary: Limits on small combatants encouraged competing destroyer philosophies, from near-cruiser gun armaments to rapid-fire destroyer weapons.
segments.body: The treaty did not prescribe a single destroyer design, but it made national choices more visible. The speakers link its pressures to large destroyers, destroyer leaders, and the later Tribal class, as navies balanced calibre, numbers of guns, and hull size. A comparison with American post-treaty discussions highlights the move toward the five-inch/38, valued for usefulness rather than maximum theoretical penetration. The wider point is that arms control redirected competition into the details of design rather than ending it.

evidence.note: The discussion connects the treaty to Tribal-class and other super-destroyer design choices.
The speakers contrast proposed American destroyer guns with the five-inch/38 and its practical virtues.

segments.title: Destroyers: rapid fire, screening, and torpedoes
segments.summary: A destroyer's main anti-capital-ship weapon was the torpedo; fast-firing guns helped it screen friendly heavy ships and fight enemy destroyers.
segments.body: This is a compact explanation of destroyer doctrine rather than simply an argument about gun size. A small, fast, moving hull was poorly suited to slow long-range precision gunnery, so rate of fire and quick changes of aim mattered more. Destroyers used that gunfire to disrupt opposing destroyers and prevent them reaching capital ships with torpedoes. Their own torpedoes remained the decisive threat against larger vessels, making an over-gunned 'small cruiser' a poor substitute for a true destroyer.

evidence.note: Rapid firing is contrasted with battleship salvos and tied to fighting enemy destroyers.
The transcript identifies torpedoes as the destroyer's anti-big-ship weapon and critiques treating it as a small cruiser.

segments.title: National destroyer solutions and the six-inch cruiser
segments.summary: British, American, Japanese, French, and Italian design choices reveal different answers to the balance between torpedoes, destroyer guns, armour, and mobility.
segments.body: The video compares national compromises rather than presenting any one fleet as uniformly correct. American and Japanese large destroyers are described as strongly torpedo-focused, while the Royal Navy pursued more destroyer gun power without losing sight of the torpedo role. French and Italian ships are presented as very large destroyer or very small cruiser designs suited especially to Mediterranean conditions. The discussion then turns to the Royal Navy's affection for six-inch cruisers: a six-inch gun could fight another cruiser without the weight penalty of an eight-inch battery, although armour was often the price paid elsewhere. Fast turret traverse and stern fire are also linked to the manoeuvring demands of torpedo attack and withdrawal.

evidence.note: The speakers compare British, American, Japanese, French, and Italian destroyer philosophies.
The transcript explains the six-inch cruiser argument, armour trade-offs, and the value of fast-traversing turrets.
Stern fire and turret movement are related to a ship running after launching torpedoes.

segments.title: Article 9 and controlled fleet replacement
segments.summary: Article 9 linked replacement to schedules, preventing a navy from rebuilding its whole fleet at once around the treaty's new limits.
segments.body: Article 9 is introduced as the rule governing the replacement of non-carrier warships up to 10,000 tons. Its practical purpose was to prevent a state from immediately discarding its existing fleet and replacing everything with vessels optimized for the new rules. That restriction also reduced the danger that rival navies would face a sudden and expensive replacement race. The speakers note that physical ships were harder to conceal than paper declarations, even if states might still shade their reported specifications.

evidence.note: Article 9's replacement rule and its scheduling purpose are discussed.
The speakers use exaggerated examples to show why fleet replacement and major ship characteristics were difficult to disguise.

segments.title: Article 10's notification and verification system
segments.summary: Article 10 required notice of a ship's laying down and completion, along with dimensions, displacement, and main-gun information that enabled outside scrutiny.
segments.body: This portion explains the information-sharing machinery behind treaty enforcement. Contracting parties had to notify one another within a month of laying down or completing covered vessels and provide classification, standard displacement, dimensions, draft, and largest-gun calibre. The speakers question how much such declarations could be trusted, but stress that naval architects could compare claims with photographs and basic calculations. Transparency therefore did not eliminate deception; it narrowed the room in which a navy could conceal a major discrepancy.

evidence.note: The treaty text lists the notices and technical particulars required for covered ships.
The speakers discuss naval intelligence, published reference works, and checking declarations against visible ship characteristics.

segments.title: A counterfactual early Japanese-American war
segments.summary: The closing counterfactual argues that without the London Treaty changes, Japan might have felt compelled to strike the United States in 1937 or 1938 rather than later.
segments.body: The speakers briefly move beyond Articles 8–10 to consider the treaty system's strategic consequences. The proposed scenario is that continued Washington Treaty limits, without the later adjustments, might have made the Imperial Japanese Navy fear an accelerating American advantage by the late 1930s. That anxiety could have encouraged a strike during Japan's 1937 expansion in China, when American Fleet Problems were concentrating the fleet around Hawaii and the central Pacific. It is explicitly a counterfactual argument, useful for thinking about how treaty constraints shaped perceptions of timing as well as ship designs.

evidence.note: The speakers frame the discussion as a counterfactual about the treaty's larger impact.
They outline a possible Japanese strike in 1937 or 1938 alongside the war in China and American fleet concentration near Hawaii.
